items.name: drained
items.close_owner: OutputBufferReader.Read (output_buffer.go:72) — correct, as Read is the sole producer of the drained signal and has the only close site

hazards.detail: OutputBufferReader.Read both receives on 'drained' at line 70 and closes it at line 72 of the same function. If the receive at line 70 is on 'drained' itself (not a different channel), Read will block forever waiting for a close that only it can perform — a self-deadlock. manager.go:170 (JobManager.Output) is also blocked on the same channel, so both goroutines hang permanently. No external closer exists.

If Read exits early (panic, early return before line 72, or context cancellation), the close at line 72 is never reached. manager.go:170 blocks on <-drained indefinitely. There is no defer-close or once.Do guard visible in the provided source, so any non-happy-path exit from Read leaks the goroutine in Output.

There is one close site, but no sync.Once or similar guard. If OutputBufferReader.Read is called concurrently by two goroutines (nothing in NewReader prevents sharing the same *OutputBufferReader), both may race to close(drained) at line 72, causing a runtime panic. Close ownership must be enforced with a sync.Once or a structural guarantee that Read is called at most once per reader instance.

items.llm_summary: The 'drained' channel carries a self-referential risk: Read both receives on it (line 70) and is the sole closer (line 72), which deadlocks if the receive is on 'drained' itself; additionally, no once-close guard protects against concurrent or repeated calls to Read causing a double-close panic, and any early exit from Read will permanently block the waiting Output goroutine.
